elements.sent-2411-001: In the maximum directed cut ($\mdcut$) problem, the input is a directed graph $G=(V,E)$, and the goal is to pick a partition $V = S \cup (V \setminus S)$ of the vertices such that as many edges as possible go from $S$ to $V\setminus S$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Oblivious algorithms, introduced by \textcite{FJ15 , are a simple class of algorithms for this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-003: These algorithms independently and randomly assign each vertex $v$ to either $S$ or $V \setminus S$, and the distribution of $v$'s assignment is determined using only extremely local information about $v$: its bias, i.e., the relative difference between its out- and in-degrees.
elements.sent-2411-004: These algorithms have natural implementations in certain graph streaming models, where they have important implications \cite{SSSV23-dicut,SSSV23-random-ordering,kallaugher2023exponential .}
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we narrow the gap between upper and lower bounds on the best approximation ratio achievable by oblivious algorithms for $\mdcut$.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that there exists an oblivious algorithm achieving an approximation ratio of at least $0.4853$, while every oblivious algorithm obeying a natural symmetry property achieves an approximation ratio of at most $0.4889$.
elements.sent-2411-007: The previous known bounds were $0.4844$ and $0.4899$, due to \textcite{Sin23-kand,FJ15 , respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Our techniques involve designing principled parameterizations of the spaces of algorithms and lower bounds and then executing computer searches through these spaces.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this work, we study a special class of algorithms, called oblivious algorithms, for a specific constraint satisfaction problem, called maximum directed cut ($\mdcut$).
elements.sent-2411-010: We first informally describe these two notions; see \cref{sec:prelim:mdcut below for formal definitions.}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Background: Directed cuts, bias, and oblivious algorithms
elements.sent-2411-011: An input instance to $\mdcut$ is a directed graph $G = ([n],E)$ (possibly with edge weights) on vertex set $[n] = \{1,\ldots,n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-012: A cut is a vector $\vecx = (x_1,\ldots,x_n) \in \{\pm 1\}^n$, and can be viewed as an assignment of a bit to each vertex in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-013: An edge $(v_1,v_2)$ is satisfied by the cut $\vecx$ if $x_{v_1 = 1$ and $x_{v_2 = 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The value of a cut is the fraction of edges it satisfies, and the value of a graph, denoted $\val_G$, is the maximum value over all cuts.
elements.sent-2411-015: To define oblivious algorithms, we first define a scalar quantity associated to each vertex in a directed graph called bias.
elements.sent-2411-016: The bias of a vertex is simply \begin{equation}\bias_G(v) \eqdef \frac{\dout_G(v)-\din_G(v)}{\dout_G(v)+\din_G(v)},\end{equation} where $\dout_G(v)$ and $\din_G(v)$ are respectively the out- and in-degrees of $v$ in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Note that $\bias_G(v)$ ranges from $+1$ ($v$ has only out-edges) to $-1$ ($v$ has only in-edges).
elements.sent-2411-018: Oblivious algorithms are a class of randomized algorithms for $\mdcut$ which ``only know'' about the bias of each vertex.
elements.sent-2411-019: More formally, an oblivious algorithm is defined by a so-called selection function $\RoundS : [-1,+1] \to [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-020: The corresponding algorithm $\Obl{\RoundS}$, given a graph $G$, produces an assignment by independently setting each vertex $v$ to equal $1$ w.p.
elements.sent-2411-021: The approximation ratio of $\Obl{\RoundS}$, denoted $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS )$, is the ratio of the expected value of this ``oblivious assignment'' to the value of the best assignment, minimized over all graphs $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Oblivious algorithms for $\mdcut$ were introduced by \textcite{FJ15 , who proved both upper and lower bounds\footnote{Note on language: In this paper, we use the usual convention that upper bounds are algorithms and lower bounds are hardness results.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Confusingly, an ``upper bound'' actually lower-bounds the maximum ratio achievable by any oblivious algorithm, while a ``lower bound'' upper-bounds this ratio.
elements.sent-2411-024: A selection function $\RoundS : [-1,+1] \to [0,1]$ is antisymmetric if for all $b \in [-1,+1]$, $\RoundS(-b) = 1-\RoundS(b)$ (see also \cref{def:antisym ).}
elements.sent-2411-025: Most of the selection functions which have been studied have this property.
elements.sent-2411-026: The constant in \cref{thm:fj-lb-general is not optimized in the work of \textcite{FJ15 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, it is straightforward to compute the best possible constant achievable with their proof technique.
elements.sent-2411-029: But equating these two quantities and solving for $\delta$ yields $\delta = 0.0099$; at this point, both quantities are roughly $0.4998$.
elements.sent-2411-030: At any other value of $\delta$, at least one of the quantities will be larger, and therefore, $0.4998$ is the optimal bound.
elements.sent-2411-031: One particularly nice feature of oblivious algorithms described by \cite{FJ15 is that (if the selection function $\RoundS$ is piecewise constant) the approximation ratio of $\Obl{\RoundS}$ can be calculated by a simple linear program (LP); indeed, this LP essentially ``encodes'' the process of minimizing the approximation ratio over all possible graphs to find the worst-case input (see \cref{thm:antisymmetric-lp below).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: A more recent work of \textcite{Sin23-kand provided an open-source python implementation of the ratio-calculating LP, and used it to improve \cref{thm:fj-ub via a more refined analysis of a similar oblivious algorithm:.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, both the works \cite{FJ15,Sin23-kand left a significant open question: \begin{question* What is the best possible approximation ratio $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS )$ which can be achieved by any oblivious algorithm $\Obl{\RoundS}$ for $\mdcut$? \end{question* As we describe in \cref{sec:motivations below, oblivious algorithms for $\mdcut$ are known to imply algorithms achieving (arbitrarily close to) the same ratio in several different streaming models \cite{SSSV23-dicut,SSSV23-random-ordering,kallaugher2023exponential .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Resolving this question would therefore characterize the best approximation ratios achievable via these streaming techniques.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this work, we make progress on the question and tighter upper and lower bounds on oblivious algorithms via intricate computer searches.
elements.sent-2411-036: We define a class of selection functions, which we call piecewise linear \emph{(PL) sigmoid functions (see \cref{def:pl-sig below).}}
elements.sent-2411-037: These functions are denoted $\RoundPLSig_b$, where $b \in [0,1]$ is an ``intercept'' parameter.
elements.sent-2411-038: We demonstrate a strictly better oblivious algorithm, using a discretization of a PL sigmoid function with a different intercept, namely, $\RoundPLSig_{149/309}$: \begin{restatable [New upper bound]{theorem {thmplsigub There exists an oblivious algorithm $\Obl{\RoundS}$ achieving an approximation ratio $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS ) \geq 0.485359$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, we observe that given the approximation ratio seems to increase and converge to a limit during finer discretization (see \cref{fig:discretization below).}
elements.sent-2411-040: In light of this, we note that the ratio in this theorem, $0.485282$, is only slightly larger than the ratio from \cref{thm:pl-ub , $0.485275$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This is heuristic evidence that the selection function $\RoundPLSig_{149/309}$ has a strictly higher approximation ratio than the selection function $\RoundPLSig_{1/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We prove another, more general, lower bound that holds against antisymmetric selection functions: \begin{restatable [Lower bound for symmetric selection]{theorem {thmsymlb For every antisymmetric selection function $\RoundS$, $\Obl{\RoundS}$ achieves an approximation ratio $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS ) \leq 0.4889$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Finally, we prove a lower bound against arbitrary (not necessarily antisymmetric) selection functions: \begin{restatable [Lower bound for general selection]{theorem {thmgenlb For every selection function $\RoundS$, $\Obl{\RoundS}$ achieves an approximation ratio $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS ) \leq 0.4955$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: In contrast, \cref{thm:fj-lb-antisym was weaker (by $0.003$), and its proof used multiple graphs, one of which had two distinct biases appearing (up to sign).}
elements.sent-2411-045: But more recently, several works have established that the existence of good oblivious algorithms for $\mdcut$ implies the existence of certain kinds of good streaming algorithms for $\mdcut$.
elements.sent-2411-046: These algorithms are given a list of the graph's directed edges as input and must output an estimate of its $\mdcut$ value.
elements.sent-2411-047: In particular, the existence of $\alpha$-approximation oblivious algorithms for $\mdcut$ is known to imply $(\alpha-\epsilon)$-approximation algorithms for all $\epsilon > 0$ in the following models:.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{tabular}{ccccc} Space Input ordering \# passes Setting Citation $O(\log n)$ Random $1$ Classical \cite{SSSV23-random-ordering} $O(\log n)$ Adversarial $2$ Classical \cite{SSSV23-random-ordering} $O(\sqrt n \polylog n)$ Adversarial $1$ Classical \cite{SSSV23-dicut} $O(\polylog n)$ Adversarial $1$ Quantum \cite{kallaugher2023exponential} \end{tabular} \caption{Streaming models into which oblivious algorithms are known to ``translate'', achieving the same approximation ratio up to arbitrarily small constants.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus, further improved oblivious algorithms, like we provide in this paper, imply further improvements in the state-of-the-art for all of these streaming models.
elements.sent-2411-049: The fact that oblivious algorithms can be ``implemented'' as streaming algorithms in these models is motivated by lower bounds known in some related models.
elements.sent-2411-050: In particular, it was known by a previous result of \textcite{CGV20 that $(4/9-\epsilon)$-approximations to $\mdcut$ can be computed using $O(\log n)$ space in a single, classical, adversarially-ordered pass, while $(4/9+\epsilon)$-approximations require $\Omega(\sqrt n)$ space for a single, classical, adversarially-ordered pass.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Thus, the fact that \textcite{FJ15 constructed oblivious algorithms achieving an approximation ratio $0.483 > 4/9$ implied that the \cite{CGV20 lower bound was ``tight'': Adjusting the model to add either polylogarithmically more space, random ordering, a second pass, or access to quantum bits yields strictly better approximations! In contrast, for $\mdcut$'s ``undirected cousin'' $\mcut$, optimal lower bounds are known even for $O(\sqrt n)$-space random-ordering algorithms and $o(n)$-space adversarial-ordering algorithms \cite{KKS15,KK19 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In the quantum setting, \textcite{kallaugher2023exponential claim that $\mdcut$ is the first discrete optimization problem with a provable exponential separation in complexity between classical and quantum algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-053: These separations are all powered by the existence of oblivious algorithms achieving a ratio strictly above $4/9$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Oblivious algorithms for $\mdcut$ have also been studied in several other areas.
elements.sent-2411-055: In mechanism design, \textcite{Luk14 showed that any monotone\footnote{An oblivious algorithm is said to be monotone if its corresponding selection function $\RoundS$ is monotone, i.e., $\RoundS(x)\le \RoundS(y)$ if and only if $x\le y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: There are $m$ players, each given a unique edge of the directed graph.
elements.sent-2411-057: A mechanism is a function that asks each player to reveal their edge and then chooses a (random) assignment of the vertices.
elements.sent-2411-058: The player may or may not reveal their edge truthfully.
elements.sent-2411-059: The utility of each player is the expected probability that their edge is satisfied by this assignment.
elements.sent-2411-060: A mechanism is said to be strategy-proof if the optimal strategy for every player is to be truthful.. for $\mdcut$.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Structure of rest of the paper
elements.sent-2411-061: In \cref{sec:pl-ub , we discuss our new selection function that achieves an approximation ratio of at least 0.485275.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In \cref{sec:plsigmoid_half , we prove \cref{thm:intercept-1/2-lb by explicitly constructing a graph (only on two vertices!) for which the $\RoundPLSig_{1/2}$ function achieves a strictly weaker than $0.485282$ approximation.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In \cref{sec:lowerbound_plsigmoid , we prove \cref{thm:pl-lb by constructing three graphs (one of which is on forty vertices!) and showing that every $\RoundPLSig$ function has an approximation ratio of at most $0.486$ on at least one of these graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We also give a detailed description of the linear program that we use to generate these graphs.
elements.sent-2411-065: In \cref{sec:lowerbound_general , we prove \cref{thm:gen-lb using a pair of graphs (a two-vertex and a four-vertex graph).}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, in \cref{sec:lowerbound_antisymmetric , we prove \cref{thm:antisym-lb and give a detailed description of the methodology we use to construct the lower bound instance.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: All code for this paper is available on GitHub at \url{https://github.com/singerng/oblivious-csps/ .}
elements.sent-2411-068: We begin with some basic notations for directed graphs and for oblivious algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-069: Oftentimes, we are interested in weighted graphs with integer weights.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this setting, it is useful to think of (multi)graphs as pairs $(V,E)$, where $E \subseteq V \times V$ is a multiset of edges (again, $(v,v) \not\in V$ for all $v \in V$); the set $E$ induces the weighted graph where $w(v_1,v_2)$ is the multiplicity of $(v_1,v_2)$ in $E$.
elements.sent-2411-071: For a vertex $v \in V$, the out-, in-, and total degrees of $v$ are \begin{equation}\dout_G(v) \eqdef \sum_{u \in V} w(v,u), \din_G(v) \eqdef \sum_{u \in V} w(u,v),  and  \deg_G(v) \eqdef \dout_G(v) + \din_G(v).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-073: We also let $m_G \eqdef \sum_{v_1 \neq v_2 \in V w(v_1,v_2)$ denote the total weight in $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The goal of the $\mdcut$ problem is to approximate $\val_G$ given $G$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Now, we consider algorithms for $\mdcut$ which estimate $\val_G$ using only the biases of vertices in $G$:.
elements.sent-2411-080: An oblivious algorithm for $\mdcut$ is defined by a selection function $\RoundS : [-1,+1] \to [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-081: The corresponding algorithm, denoted $\Obl{\RoundS}$, behaves as follows.
elements.sent-2411-082: Given a directed graph $G = (V,w)$, $\Obl{\RoundS}$ outputs \begin{equation}\Obl{\RoundS}(G) := \frac1m \sum_{v_1 \neq v_2 \in V} w(v_1, v_2) \cdot (\RoundS(\bias_G(v_1))) (1-\RoundS(\bias_G(v_2))).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: Observe that $\CA^\RoundS(G)$ equals the expected value of the random cut which assigns each (nonisolated) vertex $v$ as an independent $\Bern(\bias_G(v))$ random variable.
elements.sent-2411-085: We are interested in how good of an approximation the algorithm provides:.
elements.sent-2411-086: The approximation ratio of $\Obl{\RoundS}$ on $G$ is \begin{equation}\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS};G) \eqdef \frac{\Obl{\RoundS}(G)}{\val_G}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: The approximation ratio of $\Obl{\RoundS}$ is \begin{equation}\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS}) \eqdef \inf_{graph  G} \alpha(\Obl{\RoundS};G).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this paper, we are interested in some specific types of selection functions.
elements.sent-2411-090: Antisymmetry is a natural desideratum for selection functions for $\mdcut$, since the operation of flipping all edges (i.e., switching to the ``transpose'' weight function $w^\top(v_1,v_2) = w(v_1,v_2)$) preserves the $\mdcut$ value; this operation also negates the bias of every vertex, and so antisymmetry implies that the output of the oblivious algorithm is also preserved.
elements.sent-2411-091: Another class of selection functions we are interested in is ``piecewise constant'' functions:.
elements.sent-2411-092: A selection function $\RoundS : [-1,+1] \to [0,1]$ is $\ell$-class piecewise constant if there exists a partition of the domain $[-1,+1]$ into $\ell$ intervals $I_1,\ldots,I_\ell$, and $\ell$ values $p_1,\ldots,p_\ell$, such that $\RoundS(x) = p_i$ for all $x \in I_i$.
elements.sent-2411-093: For completeness, we include here the linear program introduced by \cite{Sin23-kand for calculating the approximation ratio of an antisymmetric piecewise constant selection function.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Let $\RoundS : [-1,+1] \to [0,1]$ be the antisymmetric selection function which maps $I_{+i}$ to $p_i$ and $I_{-i}$ to $1-p_i$ for $i \in [\ell]$ and $I_0$ to $\frac12$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Then the approximation ratio $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundS )$ achieved by $\Obl{\RoundS}$ equals the value of the following linear program: \begin{tcolorbox \begin{empheq [left=\empheqlbrace]{alignat*=3 \underset{\{w(\vecc) : \vecc \in C\} {\mathrm{minimize \quad \sum_{\vecc \in C p(\vecc) w(\vecc) \mathrm{s.t.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: We note that \textcite{FJ15 gave an alternative (but highly similar) LP which worked for arbitrary selection functions --- i.e., not just antisymmetric ones --- but required roughly twice as many variables.}
elements.sent-2411-100: In this paper, we only run the LP for antisymmetric selection functions, so we use the LP from \cite{Sin23-kand because of the cost savings.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Note that according to $\RoundPLSig_b$, vertices with bias exceeding $b$ in magnitude are assigned deterministically (i.e., they are always assigned to $+1$ or $-1$, depending on their sign), and vertices with smaller bias interpolate linearly between these two extremes.
elements.sent-2411-102: See \cref{fig:pl-sigmoid for a visual depiction of a PL sigmoid and its discretization.. \def\FIGlo{4 \def\FIGb{.5.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tikzpicture} \begin{axis}[ width=0.7\textwidth, xmin=-1.1, xmax=+1.1, ymin=-0.1, ymax=1.1, legend pos=south east ] \addplot[mark=none, red, thick] coordinates {(-1,0) (-\FIGb,0)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=*, color=red] coordinates {(0,1/2)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=o, color=red] coordinates {(\FIGb,1)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=o, color=red] coordinates {(-\FIGb,0)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=*, color=red] coordinates {(1,1)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=*, color=red] coordinates {(-1,0)}; \addplot[mark=none, red, thick] coordinates {(\FIGb,1) (1,1)}; \addplot[mark=none, black, thick] coordinates {(-\FIGb,0) (\FIGb,1)}; \addplot[mark=none, black, dashed] coordinates {(-\FIGb,-.1) (-\FIGb,1.1)}; \addplot[mark=none, black, dashed] coordinates {(\FIGb,-.1) (\FIGb,1.1)}; \foreach \i in {1,...,\FIGlo} { \pgfmathsetmacro{\smallx}{((\i-1) / \FIGlo * \FIGb} \pgfmathsetmacro{\bigx}{\i / \FIGlo * \FIGb} \pgfmathsetmacro{\samey}{1/2 + 1/8 * (2 * \i - 1) / \FIGlo / \FIGb} \addplot[only marks, mark=o, color=red] coordinates {(\smallx,\samey)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=*, color=red] coordinates {(\bigx,\samey)}; \addplot[mark=none, red, thick] coordinates {(\smallx,\samey) (\bigx, \samey)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=o, color=red] coordinates {(-\smallx,1-\samey)}; \addplot[only marks, mark=*, color=red] coordinates {(-\bigx,1-\samey)}; \addplot[mark=none, red, thick] coordinates {(-\smallx,1-\samey) (-\bigx, 1-\samey)}; } \end{axis} \end{tikzpicture} \caption{The step function $\RoundPLSig_{1/2}$ and its discretization into $\ell=5$ positive bias classes. The discretization is the function in red. The jump discontinuities are notated using standard marks: Open circles are open interval ends and closed circles are closed interval ends. The continuous (non-discretized) function $\RoundPLSig_{1/2}$ disagrees with its discretization only within the interval $[-1/2,+1/2]$ (marked by the vertical dashed line segments). The continuous function is represented by the black line segment within this interval.}\label{fig:pl-sigmoid}
elements.sent-2411-103: In the prior works \cite{FJ15,Sin23-kand , the highest approximation ratios achieved by oblivious algorithms were found by using discretized versions of $\RoundPLSig_{1/2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-104: These discretizations --- in the antisymmetric case --- are controlled by a parameter $\ell$, denoting the number of positive bias classes.
elements.sent-2411-105: We also use a finer discretization with $\ell = 251$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Most importantly, we use a different intercept parameter $b$.
elements.sent-2411-107: To choose $b$, we performed a binary search using a discretization with $\ell = 51$.
elements.sent-2411-108: We found that an intercept at $b = 149/309$ gives the best bound among the intercepts we inspected.
elements.sent-2411-109: We plug this discretization into the linear program for calculating approximation ratios for antisymmetric functions (\cref{thm:antisymmetric-lp ).}
elements.sent-2411-110: Ours is the natural discretization: We set $t_i = \frac{i {\ell-1 b$ for $i \in \{0,\ldots,\ell-1\}$, and then $t_{\ell = 1$.\footnote{{\`A la \cref{thm:antisymmetric-lp , this creates $L = 2\ell+1 = 503$ bias classes, labeled $I_{-\ell ,\ldots,I_{+\ell}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: In particular, $I_0 = [0,0]$, $I_{+i = (\frac{i-1 {\ell-1 b,\frac{i {\ell-1 b]$ for $i \in [\ell-1]$, and $i_{+\ell = (b,1]$, and $I_{-i}$ is defined symmetrically.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Evaluating the linear program in \cref{thm:antisymmetric-lp using the code in \href{https://github.com/singerng/oblivious-csps/blob/main/figures/new_algorithm.py {figures/new\_algorithm.py in the source repository, we deduce $\alpha(\Obl{\RoundPLSig_b ) \geq 0.485275$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: We suspect that even further improvements are possible by running the LP for an even finer discretization, but we do not know how to calculate the limit of infinitely fine discretization (i.e., the value of the actual step function).
elements.sent-2411-115: In this section, we present a number of lower bounds against oblivious algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-116: These bounds hold at varying levels of ``granularity'': Some are only against individual selection functions (like $\RoundPLSig_{1/2}$, constructed in the last section), while others hold against all PL sigmoid functions, all antisymmetric functions, or even all functions.
elements.sent-2411-117: However, the proofs of these theorems are united by an underlying methodology for producing candidate lower bound graphs, which combines grid search and linear programming.
elements.sent-2411-118: We discuss this methodology next before turning to the proofs of specific bounds.
elements.sent-2411-119: First, we describe our general strategy in this section for finding graphs that are hard for oblivious algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-120: This description is not strictly necessary for our proofs: One may simply take the explicit graphs we produce and verify that they have the desired lower bound properties.
elements.sent-2411-121: Also, some of the graphs are simple enough that one could have invented them by hand.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, we include this description to shed some light on where the more complex graphs come from.
elements.sent-2411-123: Also, when we can indeed prove such ``uniform'' lower bounds, the resulting quantitative bound will hold even against ensembles of oblivious algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-124: Ideally, given a set $\CC$ of oblivious algorithms, we would like to produce a graph $G$ and show that all algorithms in $\CC$ perform poorly on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-125: This is, in some sense, ``dual'' to the problem of finding good oblivious algorithms, where we want a single oblivious algorithm that performs well on all graphs.
elements.sent-2411-126: In general, we do not have strong evidence as to whether or not this dual procedure is tight, i.e., whether we can certify optimal bounds on the performance of algorithms for the classes we're interested in by coming up with a single hard graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-127: Indeed, some of our proofs instead consider a small set of graphs and prove that any oblivious algorithm in $\CC$ must perform poorly on at least one of these algorithms; we do not know whether this potentially stronger proof method is tight, either.
elements.sent-2411-129: We will be interested in graphs that contain only vertices of biases $t_1,\ldots,t_L$.
elements.sent-2411-130: For any such graph $G$, we can view a vector $\vecp \in \CP \subseteq [0,1]^L$ as an oblivious algorithm: Each vertex of bias $i$ is assigned to $1$ w.p.. $p(i)$.
elements.sent-2411-131: Our LP will output the graph which is ``worst-case'' for all algorithms in $\CP$ (i.e., such that the performance of the best algorithm in $\CP$ is minimized), among all graphs with vertices of bias $t_1,\ldots,t_L$.
elements.sent-2411-132: The cut from $\{1\} \times [\pm\ell]$ to $\{0\} \times [\pm\ell]$ satisfies weight $1$.
elements.sent-2411-133: The vertices $(0,i)$ and $(1,i)$ have bias $t_i$ (if they are nonisolated) for $i \in [\pm\ell]$.
elements.sent-2411-134: For $\vecp \in [0,1]^L$, the quantity $\sum_{v_1 = (b_1,i_1), v_2 = (b_2,i_2) \in V p(i_1) (1-p(i_2)) w(v_1,v_2)$ calculates the expected weight cut by the oblivious algorithm $\vecp$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: That this suffices to produce the worst-case graph is essentially a consequence of some reasoning due \textcite{FJ15 for a similar LP; the idea is to ``condense'' vertices into $2L$ equivalence classes based purely on their bias and their assignment in an optimal cut, and also to rescale so that the optimal cut has weight $1$.}
elements.sent-2411-136: However, to find lower bounds against concrete classes of selection functions, two issues remain.
elements.sent-2411-137: Firstly, we need to choose the biases $b_1,\ldots,b_L$.
elements.sent-2411-138: We typically do this by either fixing a small value of $L$ and performing a grid search, or by picking uniformly spaced points.
elements.sent-2411-139: Secondly, in the case where we desire to prove a bound against a large or infinite set $\CP^*$ of selection functions --- e.g., the set of all antisymmetric selection functions, or PL sigmoid selection functions --- we need to reduce the set of selection functions to a tractable size.
elements.sent-2411-140: We typically do this by discretizing the space of all selection functions in some form to form some small subset $\CP$.
elements.sent-2411-141: This does cause some additional loss in the approximation ratio, which will ideally be small.
elements.sent-2411-142: To carry out the proof, we first produce a candidate graph $G$ which holds against $\CP$, and then (ideally) show that it holds almost as well against $\CP^*$ by directly solving the maximization problem over $\CP^*$.
elements.sent-2411-143: In the case where $\CP^*$ is the set of all antisymmetric functions (or all functions), maximizing the weight of the assignment over $\CP^*$ corresponds to solving an $L$-variate quadratic optimization problem.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{tikzpicture} \node[draw, circle, fill=\FIGcolpos] (v1) at (0,\FIGpacky) {1}; \node[draw, circle, fill=\FIGcolneg] (v2) at (0,0) {2}; \path[diredge] (v1) edge[bend right=\FIGbend] node[weight] {$c$} (v2); \path[diredge] (v2) edge[bend right=\FIGbend] node[weight] {$1$} (v1); \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Parameter: $c > 1$. (Every two-vertex graph (without self-loops and with two nontrivial edges) is isomorphic to this graph up to rescaling.)}\label{fig:twovertex}

elements.sent-2411-150: For this case, we consider the graph from \cref{thm:intercept-1/2-lb with $c = 1.12916$.}

elements.sent-2411-152: The approximation ratio of $\RoundPLSig_b$ on this graph is given by \begin{equation}\frac{1.12916p^2+(1-p)^2}{1.12916} \, ,\end{equation} where $p=\RoundS(b_1)$ and $b_1 = 0.12916/2.12916$.
elements.sent-2411-153: We now argue that $1/2\le b \le 1$ implies that $\RoundPLSig_b$ gets an approximation ratio of at most $0.485282$ on this graph.
elements.sent-2411-154: Observe that the derivative of $1.12916p^2+(1-p)^2$ is $0$ at $1/2.12916\approx 0.46967$.
elements.sent-2411-155: Hence in the range $0.46967\le p \le 1$, $1.12916p^2+(1-p)^2$ is an increasing function of $p$.
elements.sent-2411-156: By the definition of $\RoundPLSig_b$, we have that $p = 1/2 + b_1/2b$ when $b\ge 1/2$.
elements.sent-2411-157: Hence, for $1/2\le b \le 1$, the largest possible approximation is achieved at $b=1/2$ and we already established that this is at most $0.485282$.
elements.sent-2411-159: The particularly simple structure of the found solution let us describe it analytically, as we do here.
elements.sent-2411-160: The graph has the property that for each $i \in \{1,\ldots,20\}$, the vertices $i$ and $i'$ have bias $b_i$.
elements.sent-2411-161: For each $i \in \{1,\ldots,18\}$, there is an edge $i \to (i+2)'$.
elements.sent-2411-162: For each $i \in \{1,\ldots,17\}$, there is an edge $(i+3)' \to i$.
elements.sent-2411-164: One may verify that the bias constraints and the edge structure together determine every edge weight up to vertex rescaling: e.g., if a vertex $v$ has bias $b$ and indegree $w$, then it must have outdegree $w\frac{1+b {1-b}$.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, scale=0.8] \foreach \i in {1,...,18} { \node[draw, circle, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=2em] (v0\i) at (\i, 0) {\(\i\)}; } \foreach \i in {3,...,20} { \node[draw, circle, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=2em] (v1\i) at (\i, 3) {\(\i'\)}; } Draw directed edges (0, i) -> (1, i+2) \foreach \i [evaluate=\i as \j using int(\i+2)] in {1,...,18} { \draw[diredge, red] (v0\i) -- (v1\j); } \foreach \i [evaluate=\i as \j using int(\i-3)] in {4,...,20} { \draw[diredge, blue] (v1\i) -- (v0\j); } \path[diredge, black] (v13) edge[bend right=30] (v01); \path[diredge, black] (v120) edge[bend left=30] (v018); \end{tikzpicture} \caption{The (unweighted version) of the graph used in Case 2 of the proof of \Cref{thm:pl-lb}.}

elements.sent-2411-166: Further, recall that vertices in $\GLp$ have biases $\{b_1,\ldots,b_{20}\}$, so we are interested in the selection probabilities $\{\RoundPLSig_b(b_i)\}_{i \in [20]}$.
elements.sent-2411-167: For each bias $b_i$, if $b_i \leq -b,$ then $\RoundPLSig_b(b_i) = 0$ and if $b_i \geq b,$ then $\RoundPLSig_b(b_i) = 1.$ Otherwise, we can write \begin{equation}\RoundPLSig_b(b_i) = \frac{b_i+b}{2b} = \frac12 + \frac{b_i}{2b},\end{equation} a linear function in $b^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-168: Hence over any interval $b \in [b_i,b_{i+1 ]$, $\val_{\GLp (\RoundPLSig_b)$ is a quadratic function of $b^{-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: We can therefore find the maximum $b^*$ of this quadratic function; if $b^*$ is in the interval, we are done, otherwise we take the maximum value over $b \in \{b_i,b_{i+1}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-170: We perform the explicit calculations for the edge-weights and the oblivious ratio in Mathematica (\href{https://github.com/singerng/oblivious-csps/blob/main/plsigmoid_lb.nb {plsigmoid\_lb.nb in the source repository) since the rational numbers have many digits.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Now, consider the graph shown in Figure \ref{fig:fourvertex with $c=\frac{1+b {1-b}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Vertices $1$ and $2$ have bias $b$ and vertices $3$ and $4$ have bias $-b$.
elements.sent-2411-174: By the definition of $\RoundPLSig_b$, it assigns $\{1,2\}\to 1, \{3,4\}\to 0$ and the corresponding cut has value $c^2-1$.
elements.sent-2411-175: On the other hand, the assignment $\{1,3\} \to 1, \{2,4\}\to0$ satisfies weight $c^2-1 + 2 \cdot 1= c^2+1$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Hence, the approximation ratio is at most $(c^2-1)/(c^2+1).$ For $b\le 0.225$, this value is less than $0.486$.
elements.sent-2411-177: We conclude that no PL sigmoid function can achieve an approximation ratio of $0.486,$ as desired.. \end{proof.}
